tldr: Headlines converge overwhelmingly on the POLITICAL frame around the Trump-Xi joint stance; three subsidiary frames cover military strikes (SECURITY_DEFENSE), enrichment sovereignty (LEGALITY), and China-Iran diplomatic coordination (EXTERNAL_REGULATION). Egypt is the most isolated bucket (0.272) by a wide margin, its headline uniquely framing nuclear possession as a geopolitical shrug rather than a crisis.
event_summary: US and Israeli strikes targeted at least six Iranian nuclear sites; ongoing US-Iran negotiations have stalled over uranium enrichment demands; Trump and Xi Jinping jointly agreed Iran cannot have nuclear weapons after their Beijing summit.

evidence.quote: Trump says he and China's Xi agree Iran cannot have nuclear weapons
Trump says he is losing patience with Iran after talks with China's Xi
US and Israeli strikes hit Iran sites tied to nuclear weapon work
Iran's uranium enrichment secures independence, peaceful atom — supreme leader
How a post-Cold War uranium program proved even old foes can work together
Chinese, Iranian diplomats meet before US-Iran nuclear talk

isolation_top.note: Egypt Independent headline ('Iran has a nuclear weapon – so what?') adopts a normative-shrug framing absent from every other bucket — treats nuclear possession as a settled fact requiring realpolitik acceptance rather than a crisis to be resolved.

Iran International leads with the strike damage (satellite imagery, nuclear weapon work sites) — a military-consequence frame that diverges sharply from the negotiation and diplomatic-agreement framing dominant across all other buckets.

Globe and Mail's historical analogy headline (post-Cold War uranium repurposing as a model for the Iran impasse) is the only solution-via-precedent frame in the corpus — no other outlet invokes historical diplomatic models.

Global Times frames the story through the China-Iran pre-talks diplomatic coordination — a process story about Chinese mediation rather than the Trump-Xi outcome that all other non-Russia buckets foreground.
Hindustan Times foregrounds Trump's dismissal of the uranium stockpile hunt as 'more for PR than anything else' — a meta-commentary frame on US sincerity that no other outlet leads with.

silences.note: Egypt Independent's headline ('Iran has a nuclear weapon – so what?') does not fit any standard Boydstun frame cleanly at the headline level — the normative-shrug register is closest to PUBLIC_OPINION but is editorial in a way that resists classification; treated as an outlier silence rather than forced into a frame.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet in the corpus to headline Iran's nuclear possession as a fait accompli requiring realpolitik acceptance ('so what?') — the most isolated bucket at 0.272 with a frame entirely absent from the remaining nine buckets.

Two TASS headlines frame enrichment as Iranian sovereignty and peaceful independence — the only outlet that directly reproduces Khamenei's framing rather than treating enrichment as a threat to be contained.
Historical-precedent headline (post-Cold War uranium repurposing) frames the Iran impasse as solvable via past US-Russia cooperation — the only constructive-analogy headline in the 13-article corpus.

bottom_line: At the headline level, nine of ten buckets frame the Iran nuclear story through Trump's political posturing (losing patience, Xi agreement, annihilation threat) or its legal/sovereignty dimensions (enrichment rights, consortium proposals). The sole outlier is Egypt, whose 'so what?' headline constitutes the starkest divergence in the corpus — a fatalist normalization frame with no parallel elsewhere. Canada's historical-analogy approach is the second most distinctive but remains within a constructive-diplomacy register.
